title: Knapsack
description: The classic 0/1 Knapsack problem: 2D DP's item-index + capacity pattern, choosing whether to take or skip each item to maximize value under a weight limit.
objectives: Solve the 0/1 Knapsack problem using 2D dynamic programming
Define a DP state combining an item index and a remaining capacity
Recognize 0/1 Knapsack as a template for many constrained-choice DP problems

sections.text: 1. Recap: Two Dimensions, Different Meaning This Time
2D DP's Unique Paths used dp[i][j] for a row and a column of a grid. Knapsack also uses two indices, but they mean something completely different: an item index, and a remaining weight capacity.
dp[i][w] represents the maximum value achievable using only the first i items, with a knapsack capacity of w.

This is House Robber's skip-or-take recurrence, generalized: instead of an adjacency restriction, the constraint is a shared weight budget across all items, so the 'take' branch must also shrink the remaining capacity.

sections.title: Why the answer is 9
points: Row 0 (no items considered yet) is all 0 — nothing to take, nothing to gain
Taking item weight=3,value=4 and item weight=4,value=5 uses exactly 3+4=7 capacity for 4+5=9 value
No other combination fitting within capacity 7 beats 9 — e.g. weight=1,value=1 + weight=5,value=7 gives only 8
dp[n][capacity] = dp[4][7] = 9 — the bottom-right corner of the table holds the final answer

sections.title: Packing a suitcase with a strict weight limit before a flight
sections.text: Deciding which items to pack into a suitcase with a strict airline weight limit, where each item is either fully packed or left behind (no cutting an item in half), is exactly 0/1 Knapsack — at every item, the choice is skip it (keep the weight budget for later) or pack it (spend some of the weight budget for its value).

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting to check weight[i] <= w before considering the 'take' branch — taking an item that doesn't fit produces a negative or invalid remaining capacity.
Mixing up dp[i-1] and dp[i] — the 'skip' and 'take' branches both read from ROW i-1 (items before item i), never from row i itself.
Allocating a (n+1) x (capacity+1) table but forgetting the extra row/column exists specifically to represent 'zero items considered' and 'zero capacity' as clean base cases.
Confusing 0/1 Knapsack (each item once) with the unbounded/fractional variants — those change the recurrence and aren't interchangeable with this template.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: In 0/1 Knapsack, what does dp[i][w] represent?
questions.answer: The maximum value achievable using only the first i items, with a knapsack capacity of w.

questions.question: What is the recurrence for 0/1 Knapsack?
questions.answer: dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], value[i] + dp[i-1][w - weight[i]]) if the item fits (weight[i] <= w), otherwise dp[i][w] = dp[i-1][w].

questions.question: How does 0/1 Knapsack's recurrence relate to House Robber's?
questions.answer: Both are a skip-or-take choice at each step, taking the better of the two options — Knapsack adds a second dimension (remaining capacity) because its constraint is a shared weight budget rather than a simple adjacency rule.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: 0/1 Knapsack extends the skip-or-take pattern from House Robber into two dimensions: dp[i][w] is the maximum value achievable using only the first i items with capacity w. At each item, either skip it (dp[i-1][w]) or, if it fits, take it (value[i] + dp[i-1][w - weight[i]]) — and dp[i][w] keeps whichever is larger. Filling the table row by row (one row per item) over all capacities from 0 to the max eventually gives the answer at dp[n][capacity]. This item-index-plus-capacity pattern is a template for many constrained-choice DP problems, not just packing a literal bag.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Knapsack
sections.text: Knapsack = House Robber with a weight budget instead of an adjacency rule. Skip it (dp[i-1][w]), or take it if it fits (value + dp[i-1][w-weight]) — keep the best.

sections.title: Partition Equal Subset Sum
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of positive integers, return true if it can be partitioned into two subsets with equal sums.
hints: Equal-sum partition is only possible if the total sum is even; if so, the problem reduces to: can SOME subset sum to exactly total/2?

That reduced question is a pure 0/1 Knapsack subset-sum variant — each number is either included or excluded (skip-or-take), with 'weight' and 'value' both equal to the number itself, and capacity = target.
dp[w] = true if some subset of the numbers seen so far sums to exactly w — this lesson's dp[i][w] table, but boolean instead of a maximized value.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Full 2D dp table: dp[i][w] = whether target sum w is achievable using the first i numbers.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n * target) for the full 2D table

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Only the previous item's row is ever needed — collapse to a single rolling row, iterated RIGHT TO LEFT to avoid reusing the same item twice within one pass.
